NKJV.1: As snow in summer and rain in harvest, So honor is not fitting for a fool.
NKJV.2: Like a flitting sparrow, like a flying swallow, So a curse without cause shall not alight.
NKJV.3: A whip for the horse, A bridle for the donkey, And a rod for the fool’s back.
NKJV.4: Do not answer a fool according to his folly, Lest you also be like him.
NKJV.5: Answer a fool according to his folly, Lest he be wise in his own eyes.
NKJV.6: He who sends a message by the hand of a fool Cuts off his own feet and drinks violence.
NKJV.7: Like the legs of the lame that hang limp Is a proverb in the mouth of fools.
NKJV.8: Like one who binds a stone in a sling Is he who gives honor to a fool.
NKJV.9: Like a thorn that goes into the hand of a drunkard Is a proverb in the mouth of fools.
NKJV.10: The great God who formed everything Gives the fool his hire and the transgressor his wages.
NKJV.11: As a dog returns to his own vomit, So a fool repeats his folly.
NKJV.12: Do you see a man wise in his own eyes? There is more hope for a fool than for him.
NKJV.13: The lazy man says, “There is a lion in the road! A fierce lion is in the streets!”
NKJV.14: As a door turns on its hinges, So does the lazy man on his bed.
NKJV.15: The lazy man buries his hand in the bowl; It wearies him to bring it back to his mouth.
NKJV.16: The lazy man is wiser in his own eyes Than seven men who can answer sensibly.
NKJV.17: He who passes by and meddles in a quarrel not his own Is like one who takes a dog by the ears.
NKJV.18: Like a madman who throws firebrands, arrows, and death,
NKJV.19: Is the man who deceives his neighbor, And says, “I was only joking!”
NKJV.20: Where there is no wood, the fire goes out; And where there is no talebearer, strife ceases.
NKJV.21: As charcoal is to burning coals, and wood to fire, So is a contentious man to kindle strife.
NKJV.22: The words of a talebearer are like tasty trifles, And they go down into the inmost body.
NKJV.23: Fervent lips with a wicked heart Are like earthenware covered with silver dross.
NKJV.24: He who hates, disguises it with his lips, And lays up deceit within himself;
NKJV.25: When he speaks kindly, do not believe him, For there are seven abominations in his heart;
NKJV.26: Though his hatred is covered by deceit, His wickedness will be revealed before the assembly.
NKJV.27: Whoever digs a pit will fall into it, And he who rolls a stone will have it roll back on him.
NKJV.28: A lying tongue hates those who are crushed by it, And a flattering mouth works ruin.

KJV.1: As snow<S>7950</S> in summer<S>7019</S>, and as rain<S>4306</S> in harvest<S>7105</S>, so honour<S>3519</S> is not seemly<S>5000</S> for a fool<S>3684</S>.
KJV.2: As the bird<S>6833</S> by wandering<S>5110</S>, as the swallow<S>1866</S> by flying<S>5774</S>, so the curse<S>7045</S> causeless<S>2600</S> shall not come<S>935</S>.
KJV.3: A whip<S>7752</S> for the horse<S>5483</S>, a bridle<S>4964</S> for the ass<S>2543</S>, and a rod<S>7626</S> for the fool's<S>3684</S> back<S>1460</S>.
KJV.4: Answer<S>6030</S> not a fool<S>3684</S> according to his folly<S>200</S>, lest thou also be like<S>7737</S> unto him.
KJV.5: Answer<S>6030</S> a fool<S>3684</S> according to his folly<S>200</S>, lest he be wise<S>2450</S> in his own conceit<S>5869</S>. <sup>conceit: Heb. eyes</sup>
KJV.6: He that sendeth<S>7971</S> a message<S>1697</S> by the hand<S>3027</S> of a fool<S>3684</S> cutteth off<S>7096</S> the feet<S>7272</S>, and drinketh<S>8354</S> damage<S>2555</S>. <sup>damage: or, violence</sup>
KJV.7: The legs<S>7785</S> of the lame<S>6455</S> are not equal<S>1809</S>: so is a parable<S>4912</S> in the mouth<S>6310</S> of fools<S>3684</S>. <sup>are: Heb. are lifted up</sup>
KJV.8: As he that bindeth<S>6872</S> <S>6887</S> a stone<S>68</S> in a sling<S>4773</S>, so is he that giveth<S>5414</S> honour<S>3519</S> to a fool<S>3684</S>. <sup>bindeth: or, putteth a precious stone in an heap of stones</sup>
KJV.9: As a thorn<S>2336</S> goeth up<S>5927</S> into the hand<S>3027</S> of a drunkard<S>7910</S>, so is a parable<S>4912</S> in the mouth<S>6310</S> of fools<S>3684</S>.
KJV.10: The great<S>7227</S> God that formed<S>2342</S> all things both rewardeth<S>7936</S> the fool<S>3684</S>, and rewardeth<S>7936</S> transgressors<S>5674</S>. <sup>The great: or, A great man grieveth all, and he hireth the fool, he hireth also transgressors</sup>
KJV.11: As a dog<S>3611</S> returneth<S>7725</S> to his vomit<S>6892</S>, so a fool<S>3684</S> returneth<S>8138</S> to his folly<S>200</S>. <sup>returneth to his folly: Heb. iterateth his folly</sup>
KJV.12: Seest<S>7200</S> thou a man<S>376</S> wise<S>2450</S> in his own conceit<S>5869</S>? there is more hope<S>8615</S> of a fool<S>3684</S> than of him.
KJV.13: The slothful<S>6102</S> man saith<S>559</S>, There is a lion<S>7826</S> in the way<S>1870</S>; a lion<S>738</S> is in the streets<S>7339</S>.
KJV.14: As the door<S>1817</S> turneth<S>5437</S> upon his hinges<S>6735</S>, so doth the slothful<S>6102</S> upon his bed<S>4296</S>.
KJV.15: The slothful<S>6102</S> hideth<S>2934</S> his hand<S>3027</S> in his bosom<S>6747</S>; it grieveth<S>3811</S> him to bring it again<S>7725</S> to his mouth<S>6310</S>. <sup>it grieveth: or, he is weary</sup>
KJV.16: The sluggard<S>6102</S> is wiser<S>2450</S> in his own conceit<S>5869</S> than seven men<S>7651</S> that can render<S>7725</S> a reason<S>2940</S>.
KJV.17: He that passeth by<S>5674</S>, and meddleth<S>5674</S> with strife<S>7379</S> belonging not to him, is like one that taketh<S>2388</S> a dog<S>3611</S> by the ears<S>241</S>. <sup>meddleth: or, is enraged</sup>
KJV.18: As a mad<S>3856</S> man who casteth<S>3384</S> firebrands<S>2131</S>, arrows<S>2671</S>, and death<S>4194</S>, <sup>firebrands: Heb. flames, or, sparks</sup>
KJV.19: So is the man<S>376</S> that deceiveth<S>7411</S> his neighbour<S>7453</S>, and saith<S>559</S>, Am not I in sport<S>7832</S>?
KJV.20: Where no<S>657</S> wood<S>6086</S> is, there the fire<S>784</S> goeth out<S>3518</S>: so where there is no talebearer<S>5372</S>, the strife<S>4066</S> ceaseth<S>8367</S>. <sup>Where no: Heb. Without wood</sup> <sup>talebearer: or, whisperer</sup> <sup>ceaseth: Heb. is silent</sup>
KJV.21: As coals<S>6352</S> are to burning coals<S>1513</S>, and wood<S>6086</S> to fire<S>784</S>; so is a contentious<S>4079</S> <S>4066</S> man<S>376</S> to kindle<S>2787</S> strife<S>7379</S>.
KJV.22: The words<S>1697</S> of a talebearer<S>5372</S> are as wounds<S>3859</S>, and they go down<S>3381</S> into the innermost parts<S>2315</S> of the belly<S>990</S>. <sup>innermost: Heb. chambers</sup>
KJV.23: Burning<S>1814</S> lips<S>8193</S> and a wicked<S>7451</S> heart<S>3820</S> are like a potsherd<S>2789</S> covered<S>6823</S> with silver<S>3701</S> dross<S>5509</S>.
KJV.24: He that hateth<S>8130</S> dissembleth<S>5234</S> with his lips<S>8193</S>, and layeth up<S>7896</S> deceit<S>4820</S> within<S>7130</S> him; <sup>dissembleth: or, is known</sup>
KJV.25: When he speaketh<S>6963</S> fair<S>2603</S>, believe<S>539</S> him not: for there are seven<S>7651</S> abominations<S>8441</S> in his heart<S>3820</S>. <sup>speaketh: Heb. maketh his voice gracious</sup>
KJV.26: Whose hatred<S>8135</S> is covered<S>3680</S> by deceit<S>4860</S>, his wickedness<S>7451</S> shall be shewed<S>1540</S> before the whole congregation<S>6951</S>. <sup>by: or, in secret</sup>
KJV.27: Whoso diggeth<S>3738</S> a pit<S>7845</S> shall fall<S>5307</S> therein: and he that rolleth<S>1556</S> a stone<S>68</S>, it will return<S>7725</S> upon him.
KJV.28: A lying<S>8267</S> tongue<S>3956</S> hateth<S>8130</S> those that are afflicted<S>1790</S> by it; and a flattering<S>2509</S> mouth<S>6310</S> worketh<S>6213</S> ruin<S>4072</S>.

ESV.1: Like snow in summer or rain in harvest, so honor is not fitting for a fool.
ESV.2: Like a sparrow in its flitting, like a swallow in its flying, a curse that is causeless does not alight.
ESV.3: A whip for the horse, a bridle for the donkey, and a rod for the back of fools.
ESV.4: Answer not a fool according to his folly, lest you be like him yourself.
ESV.5: Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he be wise in his own eyes.
ESV.6: Whoever sends a message by the hand of a fool cuts off his own feet and drinks violence.
ESV.7: Like a lame man's legs, which hang useless, is a proverb in the mouth of fools.
ESV.8: Like one who binds the stone in the sling is one who gives honor to a fool.
ESV.9: Like a thorn that goes up into the hand of a drunkard is a proverb in the mouth of fools.
ESV.10: Like an archer who wounds everyone is one who hires a passing fool or drunkard.
ESV.11: Like a dog that returns to his vomit is a fool who repeats his folly.
ESV.12: Do you see a man who is wise in his own eyes? There is more hope for a fool than for him.
ESV.13: The sluggard says, “There is a lion in the road! There is a lion in the streets!”
ESV.14: As a door turns on its hinges, so does a sluggard on his bed.
ESV.15: The sluggard buries his hand in the dish; it wears him out to bring it back to his mouth.
ESV.16: The sluggard is wiser in his own eyes than seven men who can answer sensibly.
ESV.17: Whoever meddles in a quarrel not his own is like one who takes a passing dog by the ears.
ESV.18: Like a madman who throws firebrands, arrows, and death
ESV.19: is the man who deceives his neighbor and says, “I am only joking!”
ESV.20: For lack of wood the fire goes out, and where there is no whisperer, quarreling ceases.
ESV.21: As charcoal to hot embers and wood to fire, so is a quarrelsome man for kindling strife.
ESV.22: The words of a whisperer are like delicious morsels; they go down into the inner parts of the body.
ESV.23: Like the glaze  covering an earthen vessel are fervent lips with an evil heart.
ESV.24: Whoever hates disguises himself with his lips and harbors deceit in his heart;
ESV.25: when he speaks graciously, believe him not, for there are seven abominations in his heart;
ESV.26: though his hatred be covered with deception, his wickedness will be exposed in the assembly.
ESV.27: Whoever digs a pit will fall into it, and a stone will come back on him who starts it rolling.
ESV.28: A lying tongue hates its victims, and a flattering mouth works ruin.

NASB.1: Like snow in summer and like rain in harvest, So honor is not fitting for a fool.
NASB.2: Like a sparrow in [its] flitting, like a swallow in [its] flying, So a curse without cause does not alight.
NASB.3: A whip is for the horse, a bridle for the donkey, And a rod for the back of fools.
NASB.4: Do not answer a fool according to his folly, Or you will also be like him.
NASB.5: Answer a fool as his folly [deserves], That he not be wise in his own eyes.
NASB.6: He cuts off [his own] feet [and] drinks violence Who sends a message by the hand of a fool.
NASB.7: [Like] the legs [which] are useless to the lame, So is a proverb in the mouth of fools.
NASB.8: Like one who binds a stone in a sling, So is he who gives honor to a fool.
NASB.9: [Like] a thorn [which] falls into the hand of a drunkard, So is a proverb in the mouth of fools.
NASB.10: [Like] an archer who wounds everyone, So is he who hires a fool or who hires those who pass by.
NASB.11: Like a dog that returns to its vomit Is a fool who repeats his folly.
NASB.12: Do you see a man wise in his own eyes? There is more hope for a fool than for him.
NASB.13: The sluggard says, 'There is a lion in the road! A lion is in the open square!'
NASB.14: [As] the door turns on its hinges, So [does] the sluggard on his bed.
NASB.15: The sluggard buries his hand in the dish; He is weary of bringing it to his mouth again.
NASB.16: The sluggard is wiser in his own eyes Than seven men who can give a discreet answer.
NASB.17: [Like] one who takes a dog by the ears Is he who passes by [and] meddles with strife not belonging to him.
NASB.18: Like a madman who throws Firebrands, arrows and death,
NASB.19: So is the man who deceives his neighbor, And says, 'Was I not joking?'
NASB.20: For lack of wood the fire goes out, And where there is no whisperer, contention quiets down.
NASB.21: [Like] charcoal to hot embers and wood to fire, So is a contentious man to kindle strife.
NASB.22: The words of a whisperer are like dainty morsels, And they go down into the innermost parts of the body.
NASB.23: [Like] an earthen vessel overlaid with silver dross Are burning lips and a wicked heart.
NASB.24: He who hates disguises [it] with his lips, But he lays up deceit in his heart.
NASB.25: When he speaks graciously, do not believe him, For there are seven abominations in his heart.
NASB.26: [Though his] hatred covers itself with guile, His wickedness will be revealed before the assembly.
NASB.27: He who digs a pit will fall into it, And he who rolls a stone, it will come back on him.
NASB.28: A lying tongue hates those it crushes, And a flattering mouth works ruin.

NLT.1:
Honor is no more associated with fools
than snow with summer or rain with harvest.
NLT.2:
Like a fluttering sparrow or a darting swallow,
an undeserved curse will not land on its intended victim.
NLT.3:
Guide a horse with a whip, a donkey with a bridle,
and a fool with a rod to his back!
NLT.4:
Don’t answer the foolish arguments of fools,
or you will become as foolish as they are.
NLT.5:
Be sure to answer the foolish arguments of fools,
or they will become wise in their own estimation.
NLT.6:
Trusting a fool to convey a message
is like cutting off one’s feet or drinking poison!
NLT.7:
A proverb in the mouth of a fool
is as useless as a paralyzed leg.
NLT.8:
Honoring a fool
is as foolish as tying a stone to a slingshot.
NLT.9:
A proverb in the mouth of a fool
is like a thorny branch brandished by a drunk.
NLT.10:
An employer who hires a fool or a bystander
is like an archer who shoots at random.
NLT.11:
As a dog returns to its vomit,
so a fool repeats his foolishness.
NLT.12:
There is more hope for fools
than for people who think they are wise.
NLT.13:
The lazy person claims, “There’s a lion on the road!
Yes, I’m sure there’s a lion out there!”
NLT.14:
As a door swings back and forth on its hinges,
so the lazy person turns over in bed.
NLT.15:
Lazy people take food in their hand
but don’t even lift it to their mouth.
NLT.16:
Lazy people consider themselves smarter
than seven wise counselors.
NLT.17:
Interfering in someone else’s argument
is as foolish as yanking a dog’s ears.
NLT.18:
Just as damaging
as a madman shooting a deadly weapon
NLT.19:
is someone who lies to a friend
and then says, “I was only joking.”
NLT.20:
Fire goes out without wood,
and quarrels disappear when gossip stops.
NLT.21:
A quarrelsome person starts fights
as easily as hot embers light charcoal or fire lights wood.
NLT.22:
Rumors are dainty morsels
that sink deep into one’s heart.
NLT.23:
Smooth words may hide a wicked heart,
just as a pretty glaze covers a clay pot.
NLT.24:
People may cover their hatred with pleasant words,
but they’re deceiving you.
NLT.25:
They pretend to be kind, but don’t believe them.
Their hearts are full of many evils.
NLT.26:
While their hatred may be concealed by trickery,
their wrongdoing will be exposed in public.
NLT.27:
If you set a trap for others,
you will get caught in it yourself.
If you roll a boulder down on others,
it will crush you instead.
NLT.28:
A lying tongue hates its victims,
and flattering words cause ruin.

WEB.1: Like snow in summer, and as rain in harvest, so honor is not fitting for a fool.
WEB.2: Like a fluttering sparrow, like a darting swallow, so the undeserved curse doesn’t come to rest.
WEB.3: A whip is for the horse, a bridle for the donkey, and a rod for the back of fools!
WEB.4: Don’t answer a fool according to his folly, lest you also be like him.
WEB.5: Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he be wise in his own eyes.
WEB.6: One who sends a message by the hand of a fool is cutting off feet and drinking violence.
WEB.7: Like the legs of the lame that hang loose, so is a parable in the mouth of fools.
WEB.8: As one who binds a stone in a sling, so is he who gives honor to a fool.
WEB.9: Like a thorn bush that goes into the hand of a drunkard, so is a parable in the mouth of fools.
WEB.10: As an archer who wounds all, so is he who hires a fool or he who hires those who pass by.
WEB.11: As a dog that returns to his vomit, so is a fool who repeats his folly.
WEB.12: Do you see a man wise in his own eyes? There is more hope for a fool than for him.
WEB.13: The sluggard says, “There is a lion in the road! A fierce lion roams the streets!”
WEB.14: As the door turns on its hinges, so does the sluggard on his bed.
WEB.15: The sluggard buries his hand in the dish. He is too lazy to bring it back to his mouth.
WEB.16: The sluggard is wiser in his own eyes than seven men who answer with discretion.
WEB.17: Like one who grabs a dog’s ears is one who passes by and meddles in a quarrel not his own.
WEB.18: Like a madman who shoots torches, arrows, and death,
WEB.19: is the man who deceives his neighbor and says, “Am I not joking?”
WEB.20: For lack of wood a fire goes out. Without gossip, a quarrel dies down.
WEB.21: As coals are to hot embers, and wood to fire, so is a contentious man to kindling strife.
WEB.22: The words of a whisperer are as dainty morsels, they go down into the innermost parts.
WEB.23: Like silver dross on an earthen vessel are the lips of a fervent one with an evil heart.
WEB.24: A malicious man disguises himself with his lips, but he harbors evil in his heart.
WEB.25: When his speech is charming, don’t believe him; for there are seven abominations in his heart.
WEB.26: His malice may be concealed by deception, but his wickedness will be exposed in the assembly.
WEB.27: Whoever digs a pit shall fall into it. Whoever rolls a stone, it will come back on him.
WEB.28: A lying tongue hates those it hurts; and a flattering mouth works ruin.

Smooth [3] lips with an evil heart

CSB17.25:

The one who digs a pit will fall into it,

and a flattering mouth causes ruin.

AMP.1:
Like snow in summer and like rain in harvest,
So honor is not fitting for a [shortsighted] fool.
AMP.2:
Like the sparrow in her wandering, like the swallow in her flying,
So the curse without cause does not come and alight [on the undeserving].
AMP.3:
A whip for the horse, a bridle for the donkey,
And a rod for the backs of fools [who refuse to learn].
AMP.4:
Do not answer [nor pretend to agree with the frivolous comments of] a [closed-minded] fool according to his folly,
Otherwise you, even you, will be like him.
AMP.5:
Answer [and correct the erroneous concepts of] a fool according to his folly,
Otherwise he will be wise in his own eyes [if he thinks you agree with him].
AMP.6:
He who sends a message by the hand of a fool
Cuts off his own feet (sabotages himself) and drinks the violence [it brings on himself as a consequence].
AMP.7:
Like the legs which are useless to the lame,
So is a proverb in the mouth of a fool [who cannot learn from its wisdom].
AMP.8:
Like one who [absurdly] binds a stone in a sling [making it impossible to throw],
So is he who [absurdly] gives honor to a fool.
AMP.9:
Like a thorn that goes [without being felt] into the hand of a drunken man,
So is a proverb in the mouth of a fool [who remains unaffected by its wisdom].
AMP.10:
Like a [careless] archer who [shoots arrows wildly and] wounds everyone,
So is he who hires a fool or those who [by chance just] pass by.
AMP.11:
Like a dog that returns to his vomit
Is a fool who repeats his foolishness.
AMP.12:
Do you see a man [who is unteachable and] wise in his own eyes and full of self-conceit?
There is more hope for a fool than for him.
AMP.13:
The lazy person [who is self-indulgent and relies on lame excuses] says, "There is a lion in the road!
A lion is in the open square [and if I go outside to work I will be killed]!"
AMP.14:
As the door turns on its hinges,
So does the lazy person on his bed [never getting out of it].
AMP.15:
The lazy person buries his hand in the dish [losing opportunity after opportunity];
It wearies him to bring it back to his mouth.
AMP.16:
The lazy person is wiser in his own eyes
Than seven [sensible] men who can give a discreet answer.
AMP.17:
Like one who grabs a dog by the ears [and is likely to be bitten]
Is he who, passing by, stops to meddle with a dispute that is none of his business.
AMP.18:
Like a madman who throws
Firebrands, arrows, and death,
AMP.19:
So is the man who deceives his neighbor (acquaintance, friend)
And then says, "Was I not joking?"
AMP.20:
For lack of wood the fire goes out,
And where there is no whisperer [who gossips], contention quiets down.
AMP.21:
Like charcoal to hot embers and wood to fire,
So is a contentious man to kindle strife.
AMP.22:
The words of a whisperer (gossip) are like dainty morsels [to be greedily eaten];
They go down into the innermost chambers of the body [to be remembered and mused upon].
AMP.23:
Like a [common] clay vessel covered with the silver dross [making it appear silver when it has no real value]
Are burning lips [murmuring manipulative words] and a wicked heart.
AMP.24:
He who hates, disguises it with his lips,
But he stores up deceit in his heart.
AMP.25:
When he speaks graciously and kindly [to conceal his malice], do not trust him,
For seven abominations are in his heart.
AMP.26:
Though his hatred covers itself with guile and deceit,
His malevolence will be revealed openly before the assembly.
AMP.27:
Whoever digs a pit [for another man's feet] will fall into it,
And he who rolls a stone [up a hill to do mischief], it will come back on him.
AMP.28:
A lying tongue hates those it wounds and crushes,
And a flattering mouth works ruin.

MSG.1:
We no more give honors to fools
than pray for snow in summer or rain during harvest.
MSG.2:
You have as little to fear from an undeserved curse
as from the dart of a wren or the swoop of a swallow.
MSG.3:
A whip for the racehorse, a tiller for the sailboat—
and a stick for the back of fools!
MSG.4:
Don't respond to the stupidity of a fool;
you'll only look foolish yourself.
MSG.5:
Answer a fool in simple terms
so he doesn't get a swelled head.
MSG.6:
You're only asking for trouble
when you send a message by a fool.
MSG.7:
A proverb quoted by fools
is limp as a wet noodle.
MSG.8:
Putting a fool in a place of honor
is like setting a mud brick on a marble column.
MSG.9:
To ask a moron to quote a proverb
is like putting a scalpel in the hands of a drunk.
MSG.10:
Hire a fool or a drunk
and you shoot yourself in the foot.
MSG.11:
As a dog eats its own vomit,
so fools recycle silliness.
MSG.12:
See that man who thinks he's so smart?
You can expect far more from a fool than from him.
MSG.13:
Loafers say, "It's dangerous out there!
Tigers are prowling the streets!"
and then pull the covers back over their heads.
MSG.14:
Just as a door turns on its hinges,
so a lazybones turns back over in bed.
MSG.15:
A shiftless sluggard puts his fork in the pie,
but is too lazy to lift it to his mouth.
MSG.16:
Dreamers fantasize their self-importance;
they think they are smarter
than a whole college faculty.
MSG.17:
You grab a mad dog by the ears
when you butt into a quarrel that's none of your business.
MSG.18:
People who shrug off deliberate deceptions,
saying, "I didn't mean it, I was only joking,"
MSG.19:
Are worse than careless campers
who walk away from smoldering campfires.
MSG.20:
When you run out of wood, the fire goes out;
when the gossip ends, the quarrel dies down.
MSG.21:
A quarrelsome person in a dispute
is like kerosene thrown on a fire.
MSG.22:
Listening to gossip is like eating cheap candy;
do you want junk like that in your belly?
MSG.23:
Smooth talk from an evil heart
is like glaze on cracked pottery.
MSG.24:
Your enemy shakes hands and greets you like an old friend,
all the while conniving against you.
MSG.25:
When he speaks warmly to you, don't believe him for a minute;
he's just waiting for the chance to rip you off.
MSG.26:
No matter how cunningly he conceals his malice,
eventually his evil will be exposed in public.
MSG.28:
Liars hate their victims;
flatterers sabotage trust.

NIV.1:
Like snow in summer or rain in harvest,
honor is not fitting for a fool.
NIV.2:
Like a fluttering sparrow or a darting swallow,
an undeserved curse does not come to rest.
NIV.3:
A whip for the horse, a halter for the donkey,
and a rod for the backs of fools!
NIV.4:
Do not answer a fool according to his folly,
or you will be like him yourself.
NIV.5:
Answer a fool according to his folly,
or he will be wise in his own eyes.
NIV.6:
Like cutting off one’s feet or drinking violence
is the sending of a message by the hand of a fool.
NIV.7:
Like a lame man’s legs that hang limp
is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
NIV.8:
Like tying a stone in a sling
is the giving of honor to a fool.
NIV.9:
Like a thornbush in a drunkard’s hand
is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
NIV.10:
Like an archer who wounds at random
is he who hires a fool or any passer-by.
NIV.11:
As a dog returns to its vomit,
so a fool repeats his folly.
NIV.12:
Do you see a man wise in his own eyes?
There is more hope for a fool than for him.
NIV.13:
The sluggard says, “There is a lion in the road,
a fierce lion roaming the streets!”
NIV.14:
As a door turns on its hinges,
so a sluggard turns on his bed.
NIV.15:
The sluggard buries his hand in the dish;
he is too lazy to bring it back to his mouth.
NIV.16:
The sluggard is wiser in his own eyes
than seven men who answer discreetly.
NIV.17:
Like one who seizes a dog by the ears
is a passer-by who meddles in a quarrel not his own.
NIV.18:
Like a madman shooting
firebrands or deadly arrows
NIV.19:
is a man who deceives his neighbor
and says, “I was only joking!”
NIV.20:
Without wood a fire goes out;
without gossip a quarrel dies down.
NIV.21:
As charcoal to embers and as wood to fire,
so is a quarrelsome man for kindling strife.
NIV.22:
The words of a gossip are like choice morsels;
they go down to a man’s inmost parts.
NIV.23:
Like a coating of glaze over earthenware
are fervent lips with an evil heart.
NIV.24:
A malicious man disguises himself with his lips,
but in his heart he harbors deceit.
NIV.25:
Though his speech is charming, do not believe him,
for seven abominations fill his heart.
NIV.26:
His malice may be concealed by deception,
but his wickedness will be exposed in the assembly.
NIV.27:
If a man digs a pit, he will fall into it;
if a man rolls a stone, it will roll back on him.
NIV.28:
A lying tongue hates those it hurts,
and a flattering mouth works ruin.

NRSVCE.1:
Like snow in summer or rain in harvest,
so honor is not fitting for a fool.
NRSVCE.2:
Like a sparrow in its flitting, like a swallow in its flying,
an undeserved curse goes nowhere.
NRSVCE.3:
A whip for the horse, a bridle for the donkey,
and a rod for the back of fools.
NRSVCE.4:
Do not answer fools according to their folly,
or you will be a fool yourself.
NRSVCE.5:
Answer fools according to their folly,
or they will be wise in their own eyes.
NRSVCE.6:
It is like cutting off one’s foot and drinking down violence,
to send a message by a fool.
NRSVCE.7:
The legs of a disabled person hang limp;
so does a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
NRSVCE.8:
It is like binding a stone in a sling
to give honor to a fool.
NRSVCE.9:
Like a thornbush brandished by the hand of a drunkard
is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
NRSVCE.10:
Like an archer who wounds everybody
is one who hires a passing fool or drunkard.
NRSVCE.11:
Like a dog that returns to its vomit
is a fool who reverts to his folly.
NRSVCE.12:
Do you see persons wise in their own eyes?
There is more hope for fools than for them.
NRSVCE.13:
The lazy person says, “There is a lion in the road!
There is a lion in the streets!”
NRSVCE.14:
As a door turns on its hinges,
so does a lazy person in bed.
NRSVCE.15:
The lazy person buries a hand in the dish,
and is too tired to bring it back to the mouth.
NRSVCE.16:
The lazy person is wiser in self-esteem
than seven who can answer discreetly.
NRSVCE.17:
Like somebody who takes a passing dog by the ears
is one who meddles in the quarrel of another.
NRSVCE.18: Like a maniac who shoots deadly firebrands and arrows,
NRSVCE.19:
so is one who deceives a neighbor
and says, “I am only joking!”
NRSVCE.20:
For lack of wood the fire goes out,
and where there is no whisperer, quarreling ceases.
NRSVCE.21:
As charcoal is to hot embers and wood to fire,
so is a quarrelsome person for kindling strife.
NRSVCE.22:
The words of a whisperer are like delicious morsels;
they go down into the inner parts of the body.
NRSVCE.23:
Like the glaze covering an earthen vessel
are smooth lips with an evil heart.
NRSVCE.24:
An enemy dissembles in speaking
while harboring deceit within;
NRSVCE.25:
when an enemy speaks graciously, do not believe it,
for there are seven abominations concealed within;
NRSVCE.26:
though hatred is covered with guile,
the enemy’s wickedness will be exposed in the assembly.
NRSVCE.27:
Whoever digs a pit will fall into it,
and a stone will come back on the one who starts it rolling.
NRSVCE.28:
A lying tongue hates its victims,
and a flattering mouth works ruin.

NET.1: Like snow in summer or rain in harvest, so honor is not fitting for a fool.
NET.2: Like a fluttering bird or like a flying swallow, so a curse without cause does not come to rest.
NET.3: A whip for the horse and a bridle for the donkey, and a rod for the backs of fools!
NET.4: Do not answer a fool according to his folly, lest you yourself also be like him.
NET.5: Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he be wise in his own estimation.
NET.6: Like cutting off the feet or drinking violence, so is sending a message by the hand of a fool.
NET.7: Like legs that hang limp from the lame, so is a proverb in the mouth of fools.
NET.8: Like tying a stone in a sling, so is giving honor to a fool.
NET.9: Like a thorn that goes into the hand of a drunkard, so is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
NET.10: Like an archer who wounds at random, so is the one who hires a fool or hires any passer-by.
NET.11: Like a dog that returns to its vomit, so a fool repeats his folly.
NET.12: Do you see a man wise in his own eyes? There is more hope for a fool than for him.
NET.13: The sluggard says, “There is a lion in the road! A lion in the streets!”
NET.14: Like a door that turns on its hinges, so a sluggard turns on his bed.
NET.15: The sluggard plunges his hand in the dish; he is too lazy to bring it back to his mouth.
NET.16: The sluggard is wiser in his own estimation than seven people who respond with good sense.
NET.17: Like one who grabs a wild dog by the ears, so is the person passing by who becomes furious over a quarrel not his own.
NET.18: Like a madman who shoots firebrands and deadly arrows,
NET.19: so is a person who deceives his neighbor, and says, “Was I not only joking?”
NET.20: Where there is no wood, a fire goes out, and where there is no gossip, contention ceases.
NET.21: Like charcoal is to burning coals, and wood to fire, so is a contentious person to kindle strife.
NET.22: The words of a gossip are like delicious morsels; they go down into a person’s innermost being.
NET.23: Like a coating of glaze over earthenware are fervent lips with an evil heart.
NET.24: The one who hates others disguises it with his lips, but he stores up deceit within him.
NET.25: When he speaks graciously, do not believe him, for there are seven abominations within him.
NET.26: Though his hatred may be concealed by deceit, his evil will be uncovered in the assembly.
NET.27: The one who digs a pit will fall into it; the one who rolls a stone — it will come back on him.
NET.28: A lying tongue hates those crushed by it, and a flattering mouth works ruin.
